bibliographic_data.title: Byzantine-Resilient Stochastic Gradient Descent for Distributed Learning: A Lipschitz-Inspired Coordinate-wise Median Approach
bibliographic_data.abstract: In this work, we consider the resilience of distributed algorithms based on stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in distributed learning with potentially Byzantine attackers. Toward this end, we propose a new Lipschitz-inspired coordinate-wise median approach (LICM-SGD) to mitigate Byzantine attacks. We show that our LICM-SGD algorithm can resist up to half of the workers being Byzantine attackers, while still converging almost surely to a stationary region in non-convex settings. Also, our LICM-SGD method does not require any information about the number of attackers and the Lipschitz constant, which makes it attractive for practical implementations. Moreover, our LICM-SGD method enjoys the optimal O(md) computational time-complexity in the sense that the time-complexity is the same as that of the standard SGD under no attacks. We conduct extensive experiments to show that our LICM-SGD algorithm consistently outperforms existing methods in training multi-class logistic regression and convolutional neural networks with MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets. In our experiments, LICM-SGD also achieves a much faster running time thanks to its low computational time-complexity.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a new Byzantine-resilient SGD-based optimization algorithm (LICM-SGD) that can resist up to half of the workers being Byzantine attackers without requiring any information about the number of attackers and the Lipschitz constant.
core_content.key_methodology: The method involves developing a Lipschitz-inspired coordinate-wise stochastic gradient screening and aggregation mechanism. The approach is designed to screen out noisy gradients generated by Byzantine attackers while maintaining optimal computational complexity.
outcomes: LICM-SGD can resist up to half of the workers being Byzantine attackers while converging almost surely to a stationary region in non-convex settings.
The method does not require any information about the number of attackers and the Lipschitz constant, making it practical for implementation.
LICM-SGD enjoys optimal O(md) computational time-complexity, which is the same as standard SGD under no attacks.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this work, we consider the resilience of distributed algorithms based on stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in distributed learning with potentially Byzantine attackers. To address this challenge, we propose a new Lipschitz-inspired coordinate-wise median approach (LICM-SGD). The method involves developing a Lipschitz-inspired coordinate-wise stochastic gradient screening and aggregation mechanism to screen out noisy gradients generated by Byzantine attackers while maintaining optimal computational complexity. Our main technical results show that LICM-SGD can resist up to half of the workers being Byzantine attackers, converging almost surely to a stationary region in non-convex settings without requiring any information about the number of attackers and the Lipschitz constant. Additionally, our method enjoys the optimal O(md) computational time-complexity, which is the same as standard SGD under no attacks. Extensive experiments on multi-class logistic regression and convolutional neural networks with MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets demonstrate that LICM-SGD consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of classification accuracy and running time.
